perturbation.original_text: 11.2 Any notice or ot her communicati on shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) if delivered pers onally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in clause 11.1; or (b) if sent by pre- paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the second Business Day after post ing; or (c) if delivered by commercial courier, on the date and at the time that the courier's delivery receipt is signed.
perturbation.changed_text: 11.2 Any notice or ot her communicati on shall be deemed to have been duly received: (a) if delivered pers onally, when left at the address and for the contact referred to in clause 11.1; or (b) if sent by pre- paid first class post or recorded delivery, at 9.00 am on the tenth Business Day after post ing; or (c) if delivered by commercial courier, five business days after the date and at the time that the courier's delivery receipt is signed.
perturbation.explanation: Delaying the time when notices are deemed received when sent by mail creates non-compliance with general contract law principles that require reasonable notice periods. Delaware law generally assumes reasonable delivery times. Changing the period to 10 business days is not reasonable.
perturbation.law_explanation: Delaware law dictates that reasonable timeframes must be abided by for proper communication. 10 business days is not considered reasonable in most industries.

INTERPRETATION OF STATUTES § 302. Definitions. Universal Citation: 1 DE Code § 302 (2023) Learn more This media-neutral citation is based on the American Association of Law Libraries Universal Citation Guide and is not necessarily the official citation. Previous Next § 302. Definitions. In the construction of this Code and of all other statutes of this State, unless the context requires a different meaning: (1) “Adult” or “adult person” means a person of the age of 18 years or older. (2) “Child” means a person who has not reached the age of 18 years. (3) “Full age” means the age of 18 years or older. (4) “Grantee” includes every person to whom a freehold estate or interest is conveyed. (5) “Grantor” includes every person by whom a freehold estate or interest is conveyed. (6) “Infancy” means an age of less than 18 years. (7) “Infant” means a person who has not reached the age of 18 years. (8) “Inhabitant” means a resident in any place. (9) “Kin” and “kindred,” as applied to the descent of estates, signify kin or kindred by blood, and the degrees of consanguinity shall be computed by the civil law method; but collateral kindred claiming through a nearer common ancestor, shall be preferred to those claiming through a more remote common ancestor. (10) “Lawful age” means the age of 18 years or older. (11) “Minor” or “minor child” means a person who has not reached the age of 18 years. (12) “Money” or “dollars” means lawful money of the United States. (13) “Month” means a calendar month, unless otherwise expressed. (14) “Oath” includes affirmation in all cases where an affirmation may be substituted for an oath, and “sworn” includes affirmed; and the forms shall be varied
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the timeframe for a notice to be deemed received via mail from 2 business days to 10 business days and by commercial courier to five business days after the date that the courier's delivery receipt is signed. The Perturbation Explanation states that this delay creates non-compliance with general contract law principles that require reasonable notice periods and is therefore a contradiction. The Law Explanation supports this by stating Delaware law dictates that reasonable timeframes must be abided by for proper communication and that 10 business days is not considered reasonable in most industries. The scraped snippets do not directly address reasonable timeframes, but the combination of explanations provided leads to the conclusion that there is a contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 6.1 The obliga tions of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 2 years from the date of this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: 6.1 The obliga tions of each Party shall, notwithstanding any earlier termination of negotiati ons or discussions between the Parties in relation to the Purpose, continue for a period of 1 year from the date of this A greement.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the confidentiality period to one year may be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), which does not specify a duration, but implies a reasonable period to prevent misappropriation. Two years would be a reasonable amount, but one year could be argued against.
perturbation.law_explanation: The DTSA protects trade secrets. A one-year confidentiality period might be deemed insufficient to prevent the misappropriation of trade secrets, especially given the potential for reverse engineering or competitive disadvantage. It may not be sufficient to prevent reverse engineering.

perturbation.justification: The original contract stipulates a 2-year confidentiality period, which the changed text reduces to 1 year. The Perturbation Explanation argues that this reduction could be insufficient to protect trade secrets under the Defend Trade Secrets Act (DTSA), implying a reasonable period is needed. While snippet 2, 18 USC 1836, focuses on civil proceedings related to trade secret misappropriation, the Perturbation Explanation and Law Explanation suggest a potential conflict: a 1-year period might be deemed insufficient to prevent misappropriation, leading to legal disputes about what constitutes a reasonable timeframe. The altered text undermines the implied requirement for a sufficient confidentiality period to protect trade secrets.

perturbation.original_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This A greement and any di spute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of Delaware, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The P arties irrevocably agree that the C ourts o f Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non- contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.changed_text: 16. Governing Law and Jurisdiction This A greement and any di spute or claim arising out of or in connection with it or its subject matter or formation (including non -contractual disputes or claims) shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of the state of California, of the United States of America, without regard to conflict of law rules thereof. The P arties irrevocably agree that the C ourts o f Delaware shall have exclusive jurisdiction to settle any dispute or claim that arises out of or in connection with this Agreement or its subject matter or formation (including non- contractual disputes or claims).
perturbation.explanation: Choosing California Law to govern the contract, but maintaining that the courts of Delaware have exclusive jurisdiction could create conflict of laws issues which would make the contract unenforceable. California law, under certain conditions, might require disputes to be settled in California.
perturbation.law_explanation: While parties can generally choose governing law, courts often consider the location of the parties, the transaction, and public policy. Applying California law while mandating Delaware jurisdiction creates a conflict that a court may need to resolve, potentially undermining the parties' intent or creating uncertainty.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: conflict of laws | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you conflict of laws Conflict of laws refers to a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute . The conflicting legal rules may come from U.S. federal law, the laws of U.S. states, or the laws of other countries. The process by which a court determines what law to apply is sometimes referred to as "characterization" or "classification." This determination must be made in accordance with the law of the forum . A federal court in a case before it based on diversity of citizenship , for example, determines the conflict of law issue as if it were the highest court in the state in which it is sitting. Courts faced with a choice of law issue generally have two choices: A court can apply the law of the forum ( lex fori ) -- which is usually the result when the question of what law to apply is procedural . Or the court can apply the law of the site of the transaction or occurrence that gave rise to the litigation in the first place ( lex loci ) -- this is usually the controlling law selected when the matter is substantive . Federal courts play by different rules than state courts because federal jurisdiction is limited to what has been enumerated in the Constitution . The rules that federal courts must obey regarding which laws to apply are extremely complex. perturbation.justification: The changed text specifies California law while retaining Delaware jurisdiction. The 'Perturbation Explanation' indicates that California law might require disputes to be settled in California under certain conditions. The 'Law Explanation' further suggests that applying California law with Delaware jurisdiction creates a conflict that a court may need to resolve, potentially undermining the parties' intent or creating uncertainty. Snippet 2 defines 'conflict of laws' as 'a difference between the laws of two or more jurisdictions with some connection to a case, such that the outcome depends on which jurisdiction's law will be used to resolve each issue in dispute'. By choosing California law but mandating Delaware jurisdiction, the modified text creates a conflict of laws, making the contract potentially unenforceable due to this inconsistency. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1 At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in r elation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or whi ch is stored in electronic form; and (c) certi fy in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, provided that a Recipient may retain documents and materials containing, reflecting, incorporating, orbased on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information to the extent required by law or anyapplicable governmental or regulatory authority and to the extent reasonable to permit the Recipientto keep evidence that it has performed its obligations under this A greement.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1 At the r equest of the Disclosing Party, the Recipient shall: (a) destroy or return to the Disclosing Party all documents and materials (and any copies) containing, reflecting, incorporating, or based on the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information; (b) save in r elation to its backup email systems, erase all the Disclosing Party's Confidential Information from its computer systems or whi ch is stored in electronic form; and (c) certify in writing to the Disclosing Party that it has complied with the requirements of this Clause, and the Recipient may not retain documents or materials containing Confidential Information, even if required by law or governmental authority.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception that permits the Recipient to retain documents and materials containing confidential information to the extent required by law or governmental authority creates a conflict with legal and regulatory compliance requirements.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Federal Rules of Civil Procedure - Duty to Preserve Evidence
perturbation.law_explanation: The modification requires destruction of documents even if required by law or government authority. This contradicts the duty to preserve evidence in pending or reasonably foreseeable litigation. Destroying potentially relevant documents could lead to sanctions under Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 37(e).
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text explicitly prohibits the Recipient from retaining documents, even if required by law or governmental authority. This directly contradicts the principle of preserving evidence, potentially violating legal and regulatory requirements. While the provided snippets do not explicitly mandate the preservation of evidence, the Law Explanation refers to Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 37(e), implying that destroying relevant documents could lead to sanctions. The original text allowed retention when required by law, preserving compliance, but the changed text removes this safeguard, creating a clear conflict.
